steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The hypothesis is that Ag-NP bioaccumulation in C. teleta (worms) could serve as a proxy for bioaccumulation in vertebrates like Atlantic salmon — meaning worm results could stand in for salmon results. A supporting finding must show that the two species actually behave the same way when exposed to Ag-NPs, so testing worms gives you reliable information about salmon.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "When *C. teleta* and *S. salar* are exposed to similar levels of Ag-NPs, individuals of the two species tend to accumulate similar amounts of Ag-NPs, adjusted for body size.": This directly establishes the core requirement for a proxy: if worms and salmon accumulate comparable amounts under similar exposure, then worm data predicts salmon data. The hypothesis is supported. ✓
  • "Ag-NP concentrations in *C. teleta* correlate strongly with Ag-NP levels in the environment, whereas Ag-NP concentrations in *S. salar* are fairly stable regardless of environment.": This shows the two species behave differently — worms respond to environmental levels but salmon don't. That would undermine the proxy hypothesis, not support it. ✗
  • "Compared with *S. salar*, *C. teleta* can tolerate significantly higher Ag-NP concentrations without displaying any negative effects.": This is about tolerance to Ag-NPs, not about whether bioaccumulation levels in both species match. Different tolerances don't tell us whether worm accumulation tracks salmon accumulation. ✗
  • "It is easier to detect low and harmless concentrations of Ag-NPs in *C. teleta* than it is to detect high and harmful concentrations of Ag-NPs in *S. salar*.": This is about detection ease, not about whether worm bioaccumulation mirrors salmon bioaccumulation. Ease of measurement is irrelevant if the two species don't accumulate Ag-NPs comparably. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
